greet.text: Angie sits up when you look in, instantly awake in the way of people raised inside security perimeters. "I wasn't asleep." She was. The quilt is printed with ducks; she has a bruised face and her father's stubborn jaw. "Your brother's nice. He looks at me like a math problem, but nice."

holding.text: She considers the question honestly, which children of laboratories do. "My father's dead. Everyone at the site is dead because of me. And the only person I know on the whole outside is a man who was paid to move me like freight." A pause. "You keep not being what they said extraction men are, though. So: better than expected."

dreams.text: Her eyes go to the window, to the dark past the yard light. "They're not dreams. Dreams are yours. These are... visits." Her fingers trace a vévé on the quilt without her noticing. "There's a place that's all bright lines, and things live there. Old ones, patient ones. One of them is a crossroads and one of them is a storm, and lately—" her voice drops "—lately there's a *new* one, and it's lonely, and it knows my name. Papa said they'd sing to me someday. He didn't say they'd *want* things."

want.text: "For me to come where they are." She says it simply, a girl reporting the weather. "Not die — nothing like that. Just... there's a war starting, or a wedding, I can't tell which, somewhere in the bright lines. And they've been getting me ready for it since before I could read." She looks up at you, dry-eyed and terribly composed. "You can't protect me from it, Turner. But I'd rather not go alone. That part you can do."

bye.text: She's asleep again before you reach the door — instantly, completely, the way she does everything — and in the dark behind you, very softly, she laughs at something no one in the house has said.
